cards.tip: When the customer calls you a lifesaver, that's your cue — ask for the 5-star review right there, and treat 'I'll just wait' as an invitation to ask one more question and book the free equipment-specialist visit instead of crossing it off.
When the decision-maker isn't home, get them on the phone before you leave and lock in the comfort advisor appointment — never end a dead-system call with 'she'll call us back.'

When the customer says the price is too much, don't pack up — give one empathy line and ask one question, then offer a second option, because a customer who asked you twice to find the leak is telling you they want it fixed.

Great same-day flip — now finish the call the same way you start it: recap what you found in one sentence and ask for the 5-star review by name before you walk out, because that customer was ready to give it and you never asked.
You seed the 5-star experience at the welcome on every call — now close the loop at the wrap-up and actually ask for the review before you walk out the door.

Great rapport and evidence, but stop quoting a repair price in the yard — hold every number until you're seated with good-better-best on paper, so the customer weighs your options instead of taking your $600 anchor to the home warranty.

When a customer tells you the replacement is happening this week no matter what, re-offer the same-day comfort advisor turnover tied to their deadline instead of settling for tomorrow — and never leave without asking for the 5-star review.

Your show-and-tell diagnosis is elite — now finish the play: sit them down, present two or three written options before you leave, and when a customer mentions money early, ask about it right then instead of letting a $4-5K number hit them cold.

Never quote a price range on a part you haven't tested yet — diagnose first, and when the money objection comes, answer it with empathy plus a question about their budget instead of jumping straight to 'I'll put it on paper.'

On an obsolete R22 system that's bone dry, hold the ballpark until after diagnosis and ask how long they're staying in the home first, so you can present repair and replacement side by side instead of anchoring the visit to a $1,500 fix.
